article_title: 6 open-source programs I use instead of the paid alternatives
core_thesis: The article argues that many paid software subscriptions can be replaced by capable, free open-source alternatives, and the author shares six programs she personally uses to avoid costs and vendor lock-in, highlighting that open-source software is often sufficient for everyday tasks and can even offer unique advantages.
detailed_summary: The author, Megan Ellis, recounts her shift to open-source software, initially driven by Adobe's subscription model. She lists six open-source programs she regularly uses: GIMP (image editing alternative to Photoshop), VLC (media player), Firefox (privacy-focused browser), 7-Zip (file compression), ShareX (screenshot tool), and OBS (screen recording/streaming). For each, she explains her use case and why the open-source version meets her needs, often noting additional benefits like no trial prompts (7-Zip), support for many file formats (VLC), or privacy emphasis (Firefox). The article also includes a brief discussion in the comments where users debate alternatives like Pot Player vs VLC and defend 7-Zip's superiority over Windows' built-in tools. The author concludes that while not a hardcore open-source advocate, she appreciates the free and capable nature of these tools and plans to explore more.

entities.name: Megan Ellis
entities.role: Author and software writer at XDA Developers, sharing her personal open-source software choices.

entities.name: GIMP
entities.role: Open-source image editing program used as a free alternative to Adobe Photoshop.

entities.name: VLC
entities.role: Open-source media player supporting many file formats and streaming capabilities.

entities.name: Firefox
entities.role: Open-source web browser emphasizing privacy, used as an alternative to Chrome.

entities.name: 7-Zip
entities.role: Open-source file archiver with faster compression and more format support than WinRAR.

entities.name: ShareX
entities.role: Open-source screenshot tool with annotation and editing features.

entities.name: OBS Studio
entities.role: Open-source screen recording and streaming software used for games and meetings.

key_data_points: GIMP is a free, open-source alternative to Photoshop; the author uses it for basic edits like cropping and adjusting contrast/saturation, and it supports plugins for extended functionality (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: GIMP can be used for quick image manipulation in OSINT or social engineering pretexts).

VLC supports a wide variety of file formats and can boost volume beyond 100%; it can also stream media from self-hosted servers or remote servers (🟢 relevant to your Homelab: VLC can be integrated into a media server setup or used for streaming during engagements).

Firefox emphasizes privacy and the author switched back due to Google's Manifest V3 changes; she uses it for browser compartmentalization (🟢 relevant to your Cybersecurity: compartmentalization is a good practice for separating personal and work/red team activities).

7-Zip offers faster compression with its 7z format and is completely free, unlike WinRAR which nags for payment; it supports more file formats and faster algorithms (🟢 relevant to your Programming: efficient compression can be useful for packaging tools or logs).

ShareX provides annotation and pixelation features for screenshots, beyond what Windows Snipping Tool offers (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: useful for creating clean screenshots for reports or evidence).

OBS is free and open-source for screen recording and streaming; the author uses it for game recording and as a backup for interviews/meetings, avoiding subscription fees of video conferencing tools (🟢 relevant to your Homelab: OBS can be used for recording demos or streaming CTF events).

Commenters note that Pot Player is a closed-source alternative to VLC, but VLC remains open-source with built-in subtitle search; 7-Zip is defended as superior to Windows' built-in archiver for speed, format support, and features like volume splitting and self-extracting archives.
conclusions: Open-source software can effectively replace many paid tools for everyday tasks, saving money and avoiding vendor lock-in. The author's experience shows that even non-experts can benefit from GIMP, VLC, Firefox, 7-Zip, ShareX, and OBS.

The comment section reinforces that while alternatives exist (e.g., Pot Player), open-source options like VLC and 7-Zip offer unique advantages such as format support, speed, and community-driven features.
For users interested in privacy and customization, Firefox and ShareX provide control over data and functionality that proprietary alternatives may lack.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your interests in open-source tools, which can be leveraged in both your Red Teaming and Homelab setups. For Red Teaming, tools like GIMP (image manipulation for phishing), ShareX (screenshot annotation for reports), and OBS (recording sessions) are practical. For Homelab/DevOps, VLC (media streaming) and 7-Zip (efficient archiving) are useful. As a programmer, you might appreciate the extensibility of these tools (e.g., GIMP plugins, Firefox extensions). The article also reinforces the value of free software, aligning with your student budget. You can adopt these tools immediately to reduce costs and increase control over your software stack.
extra_advice: Consider exploring GIMP's scripting capabilities (Script-Fu or Python) to automate image processing tasks for Red Teaming, such as bulk resizing or adding watermarks for phishing campaigns. Also, set up a self-hosted Jellyfin server (mentioned in the article) to practice media streaming in your Homelab, and use OBS with a portable setup for recording engagements without leaving traces.
